title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Gig Economy on Labor Rights

texts.A: The gig economy is often marketed as 'flexibility,' but for most workers, it is simply precariousness by another name. [37]By classifying workers as 'independent contractors,' platforms evade their responsibility to provide healthcare, sick leave, and a minimum wage.[/37] [39]This model effectively transfers all the risk from the corporation to the individual.[/39] We are seeing a return to 19th-century labor conditions under the guise of 21st-century technology. Without collective bargaining, 'gig' workers are left to the mercy of an algorithm that can fire them without explanation.
texts.B: The traditional 9-to-5 model is a historical anomaly that no longer fits the diverse needs of the modern workforce. Many people, from students to parents, rely on the gig economy for the ability to work whenever and wherever they choose. [38]Forcing these platforms to adopt rigid employment structures would destroy the very flexibility that makes them attractive.[/38] [40]I share the view that some protections are needed, but we should create a 'third category' of worker that combines flexibility with a portable benefits system, rather than trying to fit a square peg in a round hole.[/40]
texts.C: The real danger of the gig economy is not the lack of benefits, but the 'gamification' of labor. [39]When workers are incentivized by badges and streaks to work longer hours for less pay, it is a form of psychological manipulation.[/39] [38]I agree that the current 'contractor' status is a legal fiction used to suppress wages.[/38] However, [40]I disagree that the solution is a 'third category.' This would only create a two-tier labor market where 'standard' employees have rights and 'gig' workers have a diluted version.[/40] We need a universal basic income that makes the 'gig' a choice, not a desperate necessity.
texts.D: The growth of platform work is a natural response to the decline of traditional manufacturing. It provides an essential 'buffer' for people during economic downturns. While I share the concern about the lack of long-term security, I believe the focus should be on 'upskilling' workers rather than over-regulating the platforms. If workers have the skills to move between different types of high-value tasks, the power dynamic shifts back in their favor. [37]The problem is not the platform; it is the lack of a modern education system that prepares people for a fluid career path.[/37]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the primary cause of gig worker vulnerability?
questions.explanation: Expert A blames the 'classification' and corporate evasion of responsibility. Expert D argues the problem is not the platform but the 'lack of a modern education system' and skills.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the need for a non-traditional legal approach to gig work?
questions.explanation: Expert B suggests a 'third category' of worker. Expert C also discusses the 'third category' idea, although they eventually disagree with its implementation, they both recognize the current system is a 'legal fiction' or 'square peg/round hole' problem.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the unfair shift of risk to the individual?
questions.explanation: Expert A says the model 'transfers all the risk... to the individual.' Expert C agrees that the contractor status is a 'legal fiction used to suppress wages' and protect the platform's profit at the worker's expense.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether a 'third category' of employment is a good idea?
questions.explanation: Expert C explicitly disagrees with the 'third category,' fearing a 'two-tier labor market.' Expert B advocates for it as the best way to combine flexibility with benefits.
